agent1_snippets.content: Amniocentesis is a commonly performed procedure for several reasons. It is performed for: Diagnostic indications Therapeutic indications

agent1_snippets.title: Polyhydramnios -- Treatment / Management -- Amnioreduction
agent1_snippets.content: Significant adverse events within 48 hours of the procedure are uncommon; if events occur, they may include preterm labor, preterm prelabor rupture of membranes, intrauterine infection, and placental abruption. [43] [44] [46] However, preterm birth, in general, is relatively common in patients requiring amnioreduction, with individuals delivering on average 3.7 weeks following their first amnioreduction and at an average gestational age of approximately 36 weeks. [43] [44]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Indications -- Therapeutic Indications
agent1_snippets.content: In hydramnios, it has a therapeutic role in relieving maternal discomfort and the instillation of intraamniotic drugs. Decompression amniocentesis in twin oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios sequence (TOPS): as it decreases the volume of amniotic fluid in the polyhydramnios sac, amniotic fluid pressures are decreased in both the sacs resulting in increased placental thickness thus improving uteroplacental circulation and improving fetal outcome. [19] It can help in amnioinfusion in oligohydramnios to prevent fetal lung hypoplasia and umbilical cord compression during labor. It is indicated for fetal blood transfusion in fetuses having severe hemolysis.
agent1_snippets.query_used:

agent1_snippets.title: Polyhydramnios -- Treatment / Management -- Amnioreduction
agent1_snippets.content: In twin gestations complicated by twin-twin transfusion syndrome, amnioreduction was historically the mainstay of treatment. However, most experts now believe that selective fetoscopic laser photocoagulation (SFLP) is a superior treatment for patients with more severe diseases; SFLP treats the root cause of twin-twin transfusion syndrome and appears to be associated with superior outcomes in some studies. [2] [45] The SMFM now recommends SFLP for patients with stages II through IV twin-twin transfusion syndrome who are less than 26 weeks of gestation. [2]
agent1_snippets.query_used:

agent1_snippets.title: Polyhydramnios -- Treatment / Management -- Amnioreduction
agent1_snippets.content: For symptomatic patients with severe polyhydramnios, amnioreduction can be considered. [2] Amnioreduction is an ultrasound-guided procedure that removes amniotic fluid through a large transabdominal needle, typically a 15-cm 20-gauge needle. [43] Typically, 1.5 and 3 L of fluid are removed during the procedure. However, amnioreduction does not address the underlying cause of excess amniotic fluid, so fluid frequently reaccumulates, limiting the procedure's efficacy. More than one procedure is required in 42% to 46% of individuals. [43] [44] Amnioreduction is generally not appropriate for asymptomatic patients or those with non-severe disease. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Personnel
agent1_snippets.content: An operator has to have enough experience of at least 20 supervised amniocentesis procedures done under supervision to do this procedure independently. Operators doing amniocentesis in multiple pregnancy situations should be experts in accurate pregnancy mapping, accurate sampling, and selective termination of the pregnancy by correctly identifying the fetus.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: There are no absolute contraindications for amniocentesis. Relative contraindications for amniocentesis are: Hepatitis B and HIV infections can be transmitted from maternal circulation to the fetus during the procedure. It should be deferred in HIV-infected patients until antiretroviral treatment is started and the viral load substantially decreases. Highly active antiretroviral therapy for HIV greatly reduces maternal to fetal transmission rates. [20] [21] [22] Decreased amniotic fluid (oligohydramnios) [23] Oral anticoagulation therapy must be stopped 48 to 72 hours before the procedure, and patients may be shifted to low molecular weight heparin.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Objectives: Identify the common indications and contraindications of performing amniocentesis. Describe the equipment required, the technique used, and the patient preparation needed for obtaining valid informed consent and performing an amniocentesis. Outline and review the possible complications associated with amniocentesis. Review interprofessional team strategies for obtaining informed consent, care coordination, and communication needed to improve clinical outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Following fetal delivery, managing hemorrhage, uterine atony, and the resulting coagulopathy (DIC) is the next step. [21] Empirical administration of packed red blood cells (pRBCs), fresh frozen plasma (FFP), and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio has been traditional. However, cryoprecipitate is preferred over FFP to reduce volume overload, given its concentrated clotting factors, including factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, and fibrinogen. Tranexamic acid may be administered for fibrinolysis. [22] Blood products are used for fluid resuscitation to mitigate the risk of volume overload. [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Treatment / Management -- Previable and periviable (<24 weeks of gestation)
agent1_snippets.content: Given these risks, the decision to proceed with abortion care versus expectant management should consider the ability of the clinicians to care for the pregnant person and their neonate, the fetal prognosis, the "views of risk" as determined by both the healthcare team and the patient, and patient preference. [45] In patients who chose abortion care, dilation and evacuation are associated with substantially lower risks of hemorrhage, infection, and retained tissue than induction of labor. [48] In patients who undergo expectant management, latency antibiotics can be considered as early as 20 0/7 weeks gestation, while GBS prophylaxis, corticosteroids, tocolysis, and magnesium sulfate are not recommended until the time when neonatal resuscitation would be considered, which is typically between 22 and 24 weeks gestation, depending on local resources. [45]

agent1_snippets.title: Twin-to-Twin Transfusion Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: There are multiple management options available once TTTS is diagnosed. These include expectant management, amnioreduction, intentional septostomy (not commonly performed currently), fetoscopic laser photocoagulation, selective reduction, and voluntary pregnancy termination. Amnioreduction is typically performed to correct the polyhydramnios to < 8 cm, can be performed at any point > 14 weeks, and can be performed once or serially. Selective reduction is not considered unless the TTTS has reached stage III or IV.

agent1_snippets.title: Amniocentesis -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Amniocentesis procedure is relatively safe, with fewer complications among experienced hands. The location of the placenta is an important factor in amniocentesis. While performing the procedure, one should try to avoid penetration of the placenta. The anterior and fundal placenta is associated with more complications, including multiple pricks and blood-stained liquor; however, it is not associated with increased fetal loss rates. [37] Passing the needle through the placenta is slightly associated with increased preterm birth rates. [38]

agent1_snippets.title: Oligohydramnios -- Treatment / Management -- Amnioinfusion
agent1_snippets.content: Serial percutaneous transabdominal amnioinfusion and continuous amnioinfusion through a port have been studied in patients with severe oligohydramnios diagnosed in the second trimester, including patients with isolated oligohydramnios, early PPROM, and fetal renal disease or bladder outlet obstruction. However, data regarding perinatal outcomes are mixed. Several randomized controlled trials showed no improvement in perinatal mortality or latency periods in patients with mid-trimester PPROM treated with serial amnioinfusion. [62] In patients with suspected LUTO, the same international consensus committee referenced above stated that serial amnioinfusion should only be offered in an experimental setting. [61]

agent1_snippets.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical and ultrasound features of nonimmune hydrops fetalis to facilitate early diagnosis. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
agent2_snippets.content: Amniotomy at delivery is classified as an obstetrics incision, representing a specific procedural approach used to artificially rupture the membranes during labor. The broader category of obstetrics incision encompasses additional child procedures, including البزل القلبي (cardiac puncture) and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniotic fluid tap). Together, these interventions illustrate the range of techniques that fall under obstetric incisions, highlighting their roles in managing both maternal and fetal conditions during pregnancy and delivery.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
agent2_snippets.content: Amnioinfusion and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) are both categorized as Antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These procedures are performed before delivery and involve the insertion of instruments or substances into the maternal‑fetal environment for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. Amnioinfusion specifically refers to the infusion of fluid into the amniotic cavity to alleviate conditions such as oligohydramnios or improve fetal monitoring, while خزعة الزغابات المشيمية involves the sampling of placental tissue to obtain fetal genetic material for early prenatal diagnosis. Both interventions are considered child procedures within the broader class of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures, highlighting their role in prenatal care and fetal assessment.

agent2_snippets.content: Amnioinfusion, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية are all classified under the category of antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures. These interventions are performed before delivery to modify, sample, or assess the amniotic environment. Amnioinfusion involves the introduction of fluid into the amniotic cavity, بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniocentesis) entails aspiration of amniotic fluid for diagnostic testing, and خزعة الزغابات المشيمية (chorionic villus sampling) involves obtaining placental tissue for genetic analysis. Recognizing that these procedures share a common therapeutic and diagnostic purpose helps clinicians select appropriate antepartum interventions and coordinate patient care.

agent2_snippets.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery.
